title: Introduction
content_preview: The introduction outlines the political and constitutional crises in Pakistan from 1947-1956 compared to the post-2008 era, highlighting how they reflect structural weaknesses in governance.

title: 1947-1956: A State Without a Stable Political Compass
content_preview: This section examines the early years of Pakistan, marked by instability due to leadership vacuum after key figures' deaths, constitutional delays relying on the 1935 Government of India Act, arbitrar

title: Leadership Vacuum post Independence
content_preview: Following Quaid-e-Azam's death in 1948 and Liaquat Ali Khan's assassination, Pakistan lacked stable leadership, resulting in four prime ministers in four years, leaving the nation rudderless.

title: Constitutional Delay and Institutional Paralysis
content_preview: Pakistan operated without a permanent constitution for nine years, depending on the 1935 Government of India Act, leading to institutional stagnation.

title: Arbitrary Executive Authority Undermined Parliament
content_preview: Governor-General Ghulam Muhammad dismissed Prime Minister Nazimuddin in 1953 despite assembly support, exemplifying executive overreach.

title: Ethnic Disunity due to Forced Provincial Mergers
content_preview: The 1955 One Unit scheme merged West Pakistan's provinces, sparking resentment in Balochistan and Sindh.

title: Judiciary Enabled Undemocratic Rule
content_preview: In the 1955 Tamizuddin Case, the Federal Court validated the dissolution of the Constituent Assembly by an unelected Governor-General.

title: Post 2008: A Democracy Captured by Power Struggles and Legal Battles
content_preview: This period features ongoing crises including civil-military tensions, judicial overreach, weak coalitions, politicized accountability, and constitutional conflicts between judiciary and executive, as

title: Civil-Military Conflict behind Democratic Facade
content_preview: The 2011 Memogate Scandal revealed civilian fears of military coups following Osama bin Laden's killing.

title: Judicial Overreach into Political Domain
content_preview: In 2017, Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif was disqualified over a minor iqama issue from the Panama leaks, viewed as politically motivated.

title: Weak and Divided Coalition Governments
content_preview: The 2022 PDM alliance faced internal contradictions, hindering consistent governance and reforms.

title: Politicized Accountability Undermines Rule of Law
content_preview: The NAB disproportionately targeted opposition leaders during PTI rule, criticized by HRCP.

title: Constitutional Conflict between Judiciary and Executive
content_preview: The 2023 deadlock on Punjab and KP elections highlighted failures in enforcing constitutional timelines due to political interference in Article 224.

title: Broader Structural Weaknesses in Governance Across Both Eras
content_preview: Common issues include lack of institutional continuity with frequent prime ministerial changes, absence of checks and balances through excessive ordinances, overdependence on unelected institutions li

title: Lack of Institutional Continuity
content_preview: Frequent weak parliamentary roots and regime changes, with 14 prime ministers since 1947, undermine political sustainability.

title: Absence of Checks and Balances
content_preview: Leaders from Ghulam Muhammad to Imran Khan excessively used ordinances.

title: Overdependence on Unelected Institutions
content_preview: The military's influence over civil decisions, such as ISPR press conferences, shapes political narratives.

title: Weak Local Government System
content_preview: Grassroots democracy is ignored despite constitutional provisions, exemplified by unheld local elections in Punjab from 2016 to 2022.

title: Critical Appraisal
content_preview: This section provides a critical evaluation of the structural weaknesses discussed across both eras.

title: Conclusion
content_preview: The conclusion summarizes the persistent governance challenges in Pakistan's political and constitutional history.
